role_hints.oews_basis: 27-4021 Photographers 50.7% of wage bill (5-digit 54192 mix, shared with 541921)
role_hints.note: On-location and studio capture (products, food, architecture, corporate) is physical; but retouching, compositing, background work and batch editing — a large share of billed hours — is already heavily AI-automated, and generative AI attacks the deliverable itself (product shots, lifestyle imagery) which belongs in terminal value.

role_hints.oews_basis: 27-4031 Camera Operators, Television, Video, and Film 2.6% + 27-2012 Producers and Directors 2.5%
role_hints.note: Corporate/brand video shoots are physical; editing, cutdowns and versioning automate heavily.
Production scheduling, bid assembly and studio ops automate; agency/brand relationships persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.0% + 43-4051 Customer Service Reps 1.9% + 41-2031 Retail Salespersons 1.9% (retail share likely reflects sibling 541921 in the shared mix)
role_hints.note: Quoting, usage-license paperwork and delivery automate; winning agency/brand accounts stays relationship work.
Digital asset processing, file prep and delivery pipelines automate near-fully.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-9061 Office Clerks 4.3% + 43-3031 Bookkeeping Clerks 2.1% + unlisted admin/production-assistant tail est. ~15%
role_hints.note: Booking, invoicing, licensing renewals and asset management automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: ASMP (American Society of Media Photographers) business surveys and advocacy materials
source_hints.why: The commercial-photography trade body; has published member research and position papers on generative AI displacing assignment work.
Overlapping membership with commercial shooters; AI-tool adoption and pricing benchmark data.

source_hints.source: Getty Images / Shutterstock investor disclosures on generative AI and stock-imagery pricing
source_hints.why: The stock-photo platforms' own generative pivots quantify how fast synthetic imagery substitutes for commissioned/stock photography — the substitution benchmark for this code's product.

source_hints.source: Wonderful Machine / commercial photography pricing surveys and 'pricing & negotiation' case archives
source_hints.why: Publishes real commercial assignment budgets (day rates + usage licensing) — the concrete pricing-model evidence.
The only realistic multiple source; commercial studios trade as main-street SDE deals — no institutional comp universe exists.

capture_questions: Commercial work is priced as day rates plus usage licensing — when AI cuts retouching/production hours and clients can generate 'good enough' imagery in-house, does the licensing component (paying for rights to human-made images) collapse first, and can any operator hold day rates against procurement teams who now have a free substitute as leverage?

Who owns the client relationship — the studio brand, or the individual photographer whose portfolio the agency/brand hired? Commercial books are notoriously personal; what does an acquirer retain when the shooter leaves?

What genuinely stops a brand from generating product/lifestyle imagery with AI instead of commissioning a shoot — is it physical-product accuracy (e-commerce shots must match the real item), advertising-claim substantiation, or just habit, and how fast is each eroding?

E-commerce volume photography (catalog/product-on-white) is high-throughput contract work — do those contracts already show AI/automation clawbacks in per-image pricing, the deflation channel a researcher would miss by looking only at creative assignment rates?
terminal_value_question: Melter candidate, sharper than portrait: the deliverable is an image whose end use is commercial persuasion, and generative AI produces that deliverable without a camera — the surviving mechanism is physical-truth requirements (e-commerce images that must depict the actual product, regulated categories where depictions must be genuine, live corporate events) versus synthetic-acceptable uses (lifestyle, concept, advertising backgrounds) where paid demand evaporates. Score by segment share: how much of this code's revenue requires a photograph OF something real, versus imagery that merely needs to look real?
special_notes: n_band is 6 firms — and the record's derivation has the odd artifact that only the 500+ class lands in band (avg $13.1M revenue, implied $2.0M EBITDA) while the 100-499 class falls below it; the roll-up universe here is essentially nonexistent. Score N honestly rather than inventing a target set.

EBITDA normalization: 4,417 of 4,902 firms are <5 employees — SDE with the owner-shooter's labor inside; replacement-cost adjustment typically erases margin. Nonemployer freelancers (large multiple of employer firms) set market prices but sit outside SUSB counts.

Role-mix caveat: the 5-digit 54192 mix is shared with (and numerically dominated by) portrait studios 541921 — retail-sales and process-worker shares reflect portrait operations more than commercial studios; commercial firms carry more production assistants, digital techs and producers in the tail.

Boundary confusion: portrait studios are 541921, video production companies are 512110 (many hybrid photo/video shops classify there), stock-photo platforms are 519130-adjacent, and real-estate media companies straddle 541922/541990 — trade data (ASMP, industry surveys) spans freelancers across all of them.

Pricing-model note: usage-based licensing is a % -of-value structure that historically let photographers capture upside; generative AI turns it into the most deflation-exposed line because the client's alternative cost dropped to ~zero. Model pi_dist with the licensing collapse explicit rather than treating day-rate stability as the whole story.
